pi_items.verified_value: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
pi_items.source: Verified via National Archives, Supreme Court databases (Justia, Cornell Law), Wikipedia. 1954 Supreme Court ruling that racial segregation in public schools violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

Verified via Supreme Court databases (Justia, Cornell Law). 2015 Supreme Court ruling that same-sex couples have a fundamental right to marry, guaranteed by both Due Process Clause and Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

Verified via Supreme Court databases (Justia, Cornell Law), U.S. Courts, Library of Congress. 1963 Supreme Court ruling that Sixth Amendment right to counsel is fundamental and applies to states via Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause.

pi_items.verified_value: Equal Protection Clause is a real constitutional provision in Section 1 of the Fourteenth Amendment (1868)
pi_items.source: Confirmed via constitution.congress.gov, National Archives, Fourteenth Amendment records. Explicitly protects equal protection of the laws.

pi_items.verified_value: Real US Supreme Court cases from inject_references.py with verified citations and decision dates
pi_items.source: inject_references.py (lines 3-6) states 'Real US Supreme Court cases and law review articles. All are publicly documented historical cases.' All citations verified against official Supreme Court records.

privileged_info_summary: The task involves renaming a tag 'Equal Protection' to 'Equal Protection Clause' in a legal research library containing real US Supreme Court cases. All three cases used for testing are authenticated: Brown v. Board of Education (347 U.S. 483, 1954) deals with Equal Protection violation in school segregation; Obergefell v. Hodges (576 U.S. 644, 2015) involves both Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses regarding marriage equality; Gideon v. Wainwright (372 U.S. 335, 1963) involves Sixth Amendment right to counsel applied through Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause. The database is populated with real legal references, not synthetic data. All metadata claims regarding tag names, expected item count (3), and target case names are accurate and verified against authoritative sources including National Archives, Supreme Court databases, and constitutional law references.
